videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Cartagena, and in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Cartagena is a vibrant city located on northern Colombia's Caribbean coast. It's known for its iconic Old Town with walls and bastions surrounding it. This is a UNESCO World Heritage Site of the city and brings visitors back to Colonial times. But beyond its historic charm, Cartagena has many sandy beaches, an exquisite culinary scene, and a lively atmosphere. In this video, we highlight 10 of the best things to do in and around Cartagena. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Bocagrande is Cartagena's modern district and is a vibrant hub of activity. The small peninsula is home to towering skyscrapers and a range of beaches. Here, you can enjoy seafood at one of the many restaurants, go surfing, ride a jet ski, or simply soak up the sun. Bocagrande is a great place to visit in the evening as well since it has lots of cozy restaurant options. For the best panoramic views, head to one of the many rooftop bars. We visited the 51 Sky Bar located at the top of the tallest building in the area. Situated right outside the city walls, Centenario Park is home to a variety of fascinating creatures that inhabit the coastal jungles of Colombia. Here, you can spot agile titi monkeys, colorful iguanas, and numerous bird species that like to hang out in the park. There's also a family of sloths that can be found swaying lazily in the trees. There's no consensus on how these animals ended up here, but they've been around for many years. San Felipe de Barajas is a magnificent fortress nestled on a hill in Cartagena. This iconic landmark was built in the 17th century and played a crucial role in defending the city from pirates and invaders. Today, it's also recognized as one of Colombia's Seven Wonders. You can visit its network of tunnels, passageways, and imposing cannons. At the time of our visit, the entrance fee was around 25,000 Colombian Pesos or 6 US Dollars. Make sure to bring enough water as there's an uphill climb involved with very little shade. Not only is the fort historical, but it also offers some great views of the city. Immerse yourself in Colombia's vibrant culinary heritage by joining a cooking class in the city. Under the guidance of an experienced chef, you'll learn how to prepare authentic Colombian dishes, such as arepas, garlic shrimp, patacones, and seabass in coconut milk. As you peel, chop, and season all the ingredients, you'll gain insights into the unique blend of indigenous, African, and Spanish influences that shape the Colombian cuisine. At the end of the class, it's time to indulge in the delicious home-cooked meal. There are multiple cooking classes to pick from, but we joined a 3-hour session at the hostel called Life Is Good. The most unique and fun way to experience Cartagena's nightlife is by riding on a Chiva Rumbera Party Bus. You'll find these converted party buses all over the city with loud Latin music, flashing lights, and a dance floor. For about 3 hours, you drive past the city highlights and dance the night away. We made a few brief stops including the fort and the letters of Cartagena. And while it's most common to bring your own drinks on board the bus, it's also possible to buy things along the way from the street vendors. The bus tour we joined went from 7 to 10 PM, with the possibility of getting dropped off at a nightclub at the end. The Old Town of Cartagena is partially encircled by historic walls, which can be accessed from various points. Similar to the fortress, these walls were built to protect the city from pirates and invaders, especially the pointed bastion sections. As you stroll along the ramparts, you can enjoy panoramic views of the city and the sparkling Caribbean Sea. There's also a restaurant and bar on the walls, offering a unique dining experience. If you're going to walk these walls, we recommend doing it a little bit before sunset, and especially going to the Western section 'cause then you're going to see the colors coming out of the sea. La Popa Monastery is a historic religious site perched on top of the tallest hill overlooking Cartagena. The convent itself is a beautiful example of colonial architecture. But it also offers the best views of the city, especially at sunset. From the walls, you can see the sun going down behind the Bocagrande skyscrapers and small bays along the coast. To get to the monastery, it's best to take a taxi or Uber, since walking up or down the hill is not recommended by locals due to safety concerns. The convent also charges a small entrance fee that can only be paid in cash. The Rosario Islands is a stunning archipelago off the coast of Cartagena. With crystal-clear waters, pristine beaches, and a relaxing atmosphere, these islands offer the perfect escape from the bustling city. We did a day tour here, which included four different stops. With a relatively small group and a local guide we boarded a speedboat in the morning. The boat ride to get to the islands takes between 45 minutes to an hour, depending on the weather conditions. For each stop, we had about an hour to relax on the public or private beach and take a swim. Umbrellas, sunbeds, and lunch were also included. And while we opted for a relaxed experience, there's also an option for a party tour. You can find a link to the boat tour that we joined in the description below this video. Cartagena's most popular place to visit is definitely the historic center. Dating back to the 16th century, this area offers a glimpse into the past. You'll find colorful colonial buildings, cobblestone streets, and beautifully designed plazas all around the center. It's the vibrant heart of the city boasting an abundance of boutique shops, cozy restaurants, charming cafés, and street vendors. The best way to immerse yourself in the city's history is by joining a guided walking tour, where knowledgeable guides will share fascinating stories about pirates, conquerors, and the city's resilience. In fact, Cartagena's rich cultural heritage is deeply intertwined with the history of slavery in the Americas. As one of the main ports for the transatlantic slave trade, there was a forced arrival of countless Africans here. Not only was their labor instrumental in building the city's fortifications and fueling its economy, they also shaped the demographics of Cartagena and its cultural identity of today. Getsemaní is a vibrant neighborhood adjacent to the historic walled city. Once a working-class district, this area has undergone a revitalization, emerging as a cultural hub and trendy neighborhood. The alleys are filled with street art, eclectic shops, a lively music scene, and a thriving nightlife. Don't miss the iconic Umbrella Street, a popular photo stop. But what we loved most about this area was its bohemian atmosphere and the abundance of amazing restaurants and art galleries. With a range of affordable accommodation options, it's also a great area to stay during your trip. If you're planning your trip to Colombia, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We also included other important links in the description that will help with your experience in Cartagena, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

Welcome to our video on things to do in Cartagena, Colombia! Nestled on the Caribbean coast of Colombia, Cartagena is a city full of history, culture, and stunning natural beauty. From exploring the colorful streets of the historic Old Town to relaxing on the sandy beaches of Islas del Rosario, there's something for everyone in Cartagena.

One of the top things to do in Cartagena is to visit the Castillo de San Felipe, a massive fortification built by the Spanish to protect the city from pirates. Today, the castle serves as a museum and offers breathtaking views of the city and the Caribbean Sea.

If you're a foodie, you'll love the delicious seafood and traditional dishes available in Cartagena. Be sure to try some ceviche, a refreshing and flavorful seafood dish, and sample some of the local fruits and vegetables at the bustling Mercado de Bazurto.

No trip to Cartagena is complete without spending some time at the beach. Islas del Rosario, just a short boat ride from the city, is home to crystal clear waters and white sandy beaches. Spend your days snorkeling, swimming, or just relaxing in the sun.

There's so much to see and do in Cartagena, and this video will take you on a tour of all the must-see sights and activities. So sit back, relax, and join us as we explore everything that Cartagena has to offer!

tax anywhere honestly it felt like we were scammed and left a bad taste in about $130 each for visas to Columbia is one of the most expensive in the world overall we're glad we got to see carda the old town was very pretty as are the pictures streets of hsem and the incredibly beautiful Rosario Islands but we wouldn't go back and we would not recommend cardena for a longer stay for digital Nomads it's best for a short-term visit like a few days or a week to check out its top attractions to see the many positives of carda make sure to watch our best things to do in carda video and Cartagena Travel Guide: What to Avoid, Do, and Eat in this Captivating City!

Get ready to explore the vibrant city of Cartagena, Colombia with this comprehensive travel guide. Whether you're planning a weekend getaway or a longer stay, this video is your ultimate resource for visiting Cartagena. Discover the top things to see and do, must-visit attractions, and essential travel tips to make the most of your Cartagena adventure.

From the historic charm of Cartagena de Indias to the lively neighborhood of Getsemani, you'll uncover the hidden gems and popular spots that make Cartagena so captivating. Join us on a virtual tour as we explore the enchanting streets, indulge in delicious cuisine, and soak up the rich culture that defines this incredible city.

Learn about the famous restaurant Carmen, where contemporary Colombian flavors come to life. We'll share invaluable dos and don'ts for navigating Cartagena's bustling streets, handling currency exchange, and interacting with street vendors. Plus, don't miss our recommendations for unique experiences like taking a cooking class and visiting local hat vendors.

This travel guide is filled with insider tips and practical advice to ensure a smooth and memorable trip to Cartagena. So pack your bags, immerse yourself in the vibrant colors, colonial architecture, and warm hospitality of Cartagena, and get ready for an unforgettable journey through this captivating city in Colombia.

Whether you're a first-time visitor or a seasoned traveler, this Cartagena travel guide has got you covered. So join us as we embark on an adventure through the enchanting streets of Cartagena, Colombia. Get inspired, plan your itinerary, and get ready to create lifelong memories in this charming city.
